title: Scalable Maximum Entropy Population Synthesis via Persistent Contrastive Divergence
abstract: Maximum entropy (MaxEnt) modelling provides a principled framework for generating synthetic populations from aggregate census data, without access to individual-level microdata. The bottleneck of existing approaches is exact expectation computation, which requires summing over the full tuple space $\cX$ and becomes infeasible for more than $K \approx 20$ categorical attributes. We propose \emph{GibbsPCDSolver}, a stochastic replacement for this computation based on Persistent Contrastive Divergence (PCD): a persistent pool of $N$ synthetic individuals is updated by Gibbs sweeps at each gradient step, providing a stochastic approximation of the model expectations without ever materialising $\cX$. We validate the approach on controlled benchmarks and on \emph{Syn-ISTAT}, a $K{=}15$ Italian demographic benchmark with analytically exact marginal targets derived from ISTAT-inspired conditional probability tables. Scaling experiments across $K \in \{12, 20, 30, 40, 50\}$ confirm that GibbsPCDSolver maintains $\MRE \in [0.010, 0.018]$ while $|\cX|$ grows eighteen orders of magnitude, with runtime scaling as $O(K)$ rather than $O(|\cX|)$. On Syn-ISTAT, GibbsPCDSolver reaches $\MRE{=}0.03$ on training constraints and -- crucially -- produces populations with effective sample size $\Neff = N$ versus $\Neff \approx 0.012\,N$ for generalised raking, an $86.8{\times}$ diversity advantage that is essential for agent-based urban simulations.

sections.heading: 1Introduction
sections.text:
Urban digital twins and agent-based city models require
synthetic populations: large collections of individual
profiles whose joint statistical distribution matches
the demographic structure of a real municipality.
In practice, the only available data are aggregate
contingency tables published by national statistical
offices—marginal distributions and low-order cross-tabulations
derived from census records.
Individual-level microdata, when they exist at all,
are subject to privacy restrictions that make them
inaccessible for most research purposes.

The maximum entropy (MaxEnt) principle provides the
canonical solution to this inference problem (Jaynes, 1957 ) : given a set of empirical marginal
targets, select the distribution over all possible
individual profiles that matches those targets while
remaining maximally non-committal about everything else.
The resulting log-linear model is fully determined by
the constraint targets, requires no microdata, and
encodes only the dependencies explicitly supported by
the data. Wu et al. ( 2018 ) formalise this framework for categorical
population synthesis, and Pachet and Zucker ( 2026 ) provide
a convex dual formulation with an efficient L-BFGS solver
and a systematic comparison with the dominant practical
baseline, generalised raking.

sections.heading: 2.1Maximum Entropy Population Synthesis
sections.text:
The problem of generating a synthetic population from aggregate
census data can be framed as a maximum entropy problem.
Given a finite tuple space 𝒳 = D 1 × ⋯ × D K \mathcal{X}=D_{1}\times\cdots\times D_{K} of categorical attributes and a set of empirical marginal
targets { α j } j = 1 m \{\alpha_{j}\}_{j=1}^{m} derived from published
contingency tables, the MaxEnt principle (Jaynes, 1957 ) selects the unique distribution over 𝒳 \mathcal{X} that matches
all targets while remaining maximally non-committal about
everything else.
The resulting model is the exponential family in eq. ˜ 3 ; its log-linear structure implies that
observed correlations are modelled only to the extent
required by the constraints.

Wu et al. ( 2018 ) formalise this framework for population synthesis
and propose an efficient iterative scaling algorithm exploiting
the sparse structure of the constraint indicator matrix.
Their experiments on US census data demonstrate that the
MaxEnt model reproduces marginal statistics faithfully and
produces synthetic populations suitable for downstream
epidemic simulation.

Pachet and Zucker ( 2026 ) substantially extend this line of work by
introducing a convex dual formulation, an L-BFGS solver, and
a systematic comparison with generalised raking.
Their central empirical contribution is a competence map :
a grid of relative accuracy over population size n n and
number of variables K K , showing that MaxEnt overtakes raking
when K ≳ 28 K\gtrsim 28 and ternary interactions are present.
The present paper extends the Pachet–Zucker framework to the
regime K > 20 K>20 where exact expectation computation is
infeasible, by replacing the exact sum in their Algorithm 1
(line 4) with a stochastic Gibbs estimate.

The exponential family also arises in neural population coding (Schneidman et al., 2006 ) and melodic style modelling (Sakellariou et al., 2017 ) , where Gibbs-based inference has
been used successfully.
The present work is the first to apply PCD to categorical
demographic synthesis.

sections.heading: 2.2Generalised Raking and IPF
sections.text:
Generalised raking (also known as iterative proportional
fitting, IPF) is a widely used baseline for population
synthesis from aggregate data (Deming and Stephan, 1940 ; Lomax and Norman, 2016 ) .
Given a fixed sample of N N individuals { x ( i ) } i = 1 N \{x^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^{N} , raking estimates non-negative weights { w i } \{w_{i}\} such that the weighted feature totals α ^ j = ∑ i w i ​ f j ​ ( x ( i ) ) \hat{\alpha}_{j}=\sum_{i}w_{i}f_{j}(x^{(i)}) match the target aggregates α j \alpha_{j} , where f j f_{j} are
typically indicator functions.
The algorithm iteratively processes each constraint,
rescaling the weights multiplicatively by a factor α j / α ^ j \alpha_{j}/\hat{\alpha}_{j} for individuals matching the
constraint, and cycles through all constraints until
convergence.

Raking has two structural limitations that motivate the
present work.

sections.heading: 2.3Deep Generative Models
sections.text:
A growing body of work applies deep generative models to
population synthesis.
Variational autoencoders (Borysov et al., 2019 ) learn a
low-dimensional latent representation of individual
profiles and generate new individuals by decoding
latent samples.
More recent approaches use normalising flows and
diffusion models to capture complex multimodal
distributions over categorical attributes (Kim and Bansal, 2023 ) .

All these methods share a common requirement: they are
trained on a microdata sample of individual-level
records.
In many national statistical contexts only full or partial
aggregate contingency tables are published, and individual
microdata are restricted or unavailable.
The MaxEnt framework, by contrast, is designed precisely
for this aggregate-only regime: it requires no individual
records and is fully determined by the published marginals.
Extending MaxEnt to large K K via PCD, rather than
adopting deep generative models, is therefore the
principled choice for our application context.
The integration of deep generative models with
aggregate-constrained synthesis is an open research
direction that we leave to future work.

sections.heading: 2.4Contrastive Divergence and Persistent CD
sections.text:
Contrastive Divergence (CD) (Hinton, 2002 ) is a
family of algorithms for estimating the gradient of the
log-likelihood of an energy-based model without explicitly
computing the partition function. For models of the form p λ ​ ( x ) ∝ exp ⁡ ( λ ⋅ F ​ ( x ) ) p_{\lambda}(x)\propto\exp(\lambda\cdot F(x)) , the
gradient of the expected log-likelihood takes the form ∇ λ 𝔼 data ​ [ log ⁡ p λ ​ ( x ) ] = 𝔼 data ​ [ F ] − 𝔼 p λ ​ [ F ] , \nabla_{\lambda}\,\mathbb{E}_{\text{data}}[\log p_{\lambda}(x)]=\mathbb{E}_{\text{data}}[F]-\mathbb{E}_{p_{\lambda}}[F], where the model expectation is generally intractable.
Standard CD- k k initialises a Gibbs chain from a data
point, runs k k steps, and uses the resulting sample to
approximate the model expectation. CD- k k introduces a
bias that decreases with k k but does not vanish in
general; in practice, even k = 1 k{=}1 often works well for
restricted Boltzmann machines (Hinton, 2002 ) .

In our setting, however, no individual-level data are
available, and the "data expectation" is replaced by
target aggregate constraints. This requires adapting CD
to operate directly on the model distribution rather than
on observed samples.

Persistent Contrastive Divergence (PCD) (Tieleman, 2008 ) eliminates the reinitialisation from data by maintaining
a persistent Markov chain across parameter updates.
The chain is not reset at each gradient step;
instead, it continues from its previous state, thereby
tracking the evolving model distribution as 𝝀 \bm{\lambda} changes.

In contrast to CD- k k , this greatly reduces the bias of
the gradient estimate, since the samples are no longer
drawn from short-run Markov chains initialised at data
points. Under appropriate mixing conditions, the chain
provides samples that more closely approximate
expectations under p 𝝀 p_{\bm{\lambda}} .

PCD is closely related to Stochastic Maximum Likelihood (Younes, 1999 ) and can be analysed within the
framework of stochastic approximation. Convergence to a
local optimum can be established under standard
conditions on step sizes and mixing assumptions (Robbins and Monro, 1951 ) .

In the context of the MaxEnt population synthesis problem,
PCD approximates the intractable expectation ∑ 𝐱 ∈ 𝒳 p 𝝀 ​ ( 𝐱 ) ​ f j ​ ( 𝐱 ) \sum_{\mathbf{x}\in\mathcal{X}}p_{\bm{\lambda}}(\mathbf{x})f_{j}(\mathbf{x}) with an empirical average over a persistent Monte Carlo
sample of N N synthetic individuals.
This sample is updated across iterations and serves as a
proxy for draws from p 𝝀 p_{\bm{\lambda}} .

The population is advanced by Gibbs (Glauber) dynamics (Glauber, 1963 ; Geman and Geman, 1984 ) , exploiting the factored
conditional structure of eq. ˜ 6 , which
allows efficient local updates of individual attributes.

The partition function Z ​ ( 𝝀 ) Z(\bm{\lambda}) is never computed.

sections.heading: 3.1Setup and Notation
sections.text:
Let 𝒳 = D 1 × ⋯ × D K \mathcal{X}=D_{1}\times\cdots\times D_{K} be a finite product
space of K K categorical attributes, where attribute k k takes
values in a domain of size d k d_{k} .
A constraint j ∈ { 1 , … , m } j\in\{1,\ldots,m\} is a pattern indicator f j ​ ( 𝐱 ) = 𝟏 ​ [ 𝐱 S j = 𝐯 j ] , S j ⊆ { 1 , … , K } , 𝐯 j ∈ ∏ k ∈ S j D k , f_{j}(\mathbf{x})=\mathbf{1}\!\left[\mathbf{x}_{S_{j}}=\mathbf{v}_{j}\right],\qquad S_{j}\subseteq\{1,\ldots,K\},\;\mathbf{v}_{j}\in\prod_{k\in S_{j}}D_{k}, (2) with empirical target α j ∈ [ 0 , 1 ] \alpha_{j}\in[0,1] .
The maximum entropy (MaxEnt) distribution over 𝒳 \mathcal{X} subject to the constraints 𝔼 p ​ [ f j ] = α j \mathbb{E}_{p}[f_{j}]=\alpha_{j} for all j j is the exponential
family p 𝝀 ​ ( 𝐱 ) = 1 Z ​ ( 𝝀 ) ​ exp ⁡ ( ∑ j = 1 m λ j ​ f j ​ ( 𝐱 ) ) , Z ​ ( 𝝀 ) = ∑ 𝐱 ∈ 𝒳 exp ⁡ ( ∑ j λ j ​ f j ​ ( 𝐱 ) ) . p_{\bm{\lambda}}(\mathbf{x})=\frac{1}{Z(\bm{\lambda})}\exp\!\left(\sum_{j=1}^{m}\lambda_{j}f_{j}(\mathbf{x})\right),\qquad Z(\bm{\lambda})=\sum_{\mathbf{x}\in\mathcal{X}}\exp\!\left(\sum_{j}\lambda_{j}f_{j}(\mathbf{x})\right). (3) The Lagrange multipliers 𝝀 ∈ ℝ m \bm{\lambda}\in\mathbb{R}^{m} are the
natural parameters of the model; they are the solution of the dual optimisation problem,
which is strictly convex under standard conditions.
the dual optimisation problem min 𝝀 ∈ ℝ m ⁡ Φ ​ ( 𝝀 ) ≔ log ⁡ Z ​ ( 𝝀 ) − 𝝀 ⊤ ​ 𝜶 , \min_{\bm{\lambda}\in\mathbb{R}^{m}}\Phi(\bm{\lambda})\coloneqq\log Z(\bm{\lambda})-\bm{\lambda}^{\top}\bm{\alpha}, (4) whose gradient is ∇ Φ ​ ( 𝝀 ) = 𝜶 ^ ​ ( 𝝀 ) − 𝜶 \nabla\Phi(\bm{\lambda})=\hat{\bm{\alpha}}(\bm{\lambda})-\bm{\alpha} ,
where α ^ j ​ ( 𝝀 ) = 𝔼 p 𝝀 ​ [ f j ] = ∑ 𝐱 ∈ 𝒳 p 𝝀 ​ ( 𝐱 ) ​ f j ​ ( 𝐱 ) \hat{\alpha}_{j}(\bm{\lambda})=\mathbb{E}_{p_{\bm{\lambda}}}[f_{j}]=\sum_{\mathbf{x}\in\mathcal{X}}p_{\bm{\lambda}}(\mathbf{x})f_{j}(\mathbf{x}) are the model expectations.
The dual objective Φ \Phi is convex and, under standard
conditions, admits a unique minimiser 𝝀 ∗ \bm{\lambda}^{*} satisfying
the moment-matching conditions 𝜶 ^ ​ ( 𝝀 ∗ ) = 𝜶 \hat{\bm{\alpha}}(\bm{\lambda}^{*})=\bm{\alpha} .
In our setting, the targets 𝜶 \bm{\alpha} are specified
directly from aggregate data, and no individual-level
samples from p 𝝀 p_{\bm{\lambda}} are available.

sections.heading: 3.4Algorithmic Validation
sections.text:
We first validate that GibbsPCDSolver converges to the exact MaxEnt
solution when the latter can be computed exactly (e.g., by
enumeration for small K K ).
The three experiments below use synthetic data generated by the WuGenerator , a controlled benchmark generator we
implemented following the experimental protocol of Wu et al. ( 2018 ) ,
which constructs a ground truth as follows:
(i) each attribute k k is assigned a random marginal
distribution over D k D_{k} ;
(ii) a set of P P binary or ternary planted patterns ( S p , 𝐯 p , ν p ) (S_{p},\mathbf{v}_{p},\nu_{p}) is drawn, each with a target
frequency ν p ∈ ( 0.05 , 0.35 ) \nu_{p}\in(0.05,0.35) ;
(iii) each synthetic individual independently activates each
pattern with probability ν p \nu_{p} (Bernoulli draw), with
overlaps resolved by a fixed rule, receiving the prescribed
values for 𝐱 S p \mathbf{x}_{S_{p}} , while attributes not covered by any
activated pattern are sampled from their marginals.
The constraint set is then defined as the empirical
frequencies computed from N data N_{\text{data}} such individuals.
This procedure yields, by construction, a consistent set of
constraints with known planted structure, making it
well suited for algorithmic validation.

sections.heading: 4The Syn-ISTAT Benchmark
sections.text:
We introduce Syn-ISTAT , a synthetic demographic benchmark
designed to evaluate MaxEnt solvers in the regime where exact
enumeration is infeasible ( K = 15 K{=}15 , | 𝒳 | ≈ 1.7 × 10 8 |\mathcal{X}|\approx 1.7\times 10^{8} )
and individual microdata are unavailable.

Syn-ISTAT is an ISTAT-inspired benchmark, not a dataset
derived from official Italian microdata.
Its conditional probability tables (CPTs) reflect known
structural patterns of the Italian demographic system—the
gender gap in labour market participation, age-dependent
income profiles, the urban/rural transport split—but are
constructed synthetically to ensure full ground-truth control.
This design choice is deliberate: it allows exact analytical
computation of all marginal targets and eliminates any
confound between solver error and data inconsistency.
The application of GibbsPCDSolver to real ISTAT municipal
data and the associated
challenges of heterogeneous constraint universes and
inconsistent reference populations are left to a companion
paper currently in preparation.

Syn-ISTAT is a Bayesian network over K = 15 K{=}15 categorical
attributes representing the demographic and socioeconomic
profile of a synthetic Italian individual:
sex, age, marital status, education, employment, income,
household size, presence of children, residential area,
car access, main transport mode, commute time, diet type,
alcohol use, and physical activity
(full attribute table in appendix ˜ A ).
Six anchor variables (sex, age, education,
household size, residential area, car access) are drawn
from fixed marginals; the remaining nine are sampled from
CPTs conditioned on one or two anchor variables, forming a
shallow Bayesian network with limited dependency depth.
Three ternary CPTs encode higher-order interactions that
are invisible to any pair of binary tables: P ​ ( employment ∣ sex , age ) P(\text{employment}\mid\text{sex},\text{age}) , P ​ ( income ∣ education , employment ) P(\text{income}\mid\text{education},\text{employment}) , and P ​ ( transport ∣ area , car access ) P(\text{transport}\mid\text{area},\text{car access}) .

All marginal targets α j ∗ \alpha^{*}_{j} are computed analytically by factor marginalisation over the CPTs,
without Monte Carlo sampling.
This ensures that any solver error measured against α ∗ \alpha^{*} reflects the algorithm’s approximation, not sampling noise.
For a binary constraint ( k 1 , k 2 ) (k_{1},k_{2}) : α k 1 ​ k 2 ∗ ​ ( v 1 , v 2 ) = ∑ 𝐱 − { k 1 , k 2 } p BN ​ ( 𝐱 ) , \alpha^{*}_{k_{1}k_{2}}(v_{1},v_{2})=\sum_{\mathbf{x}_{-\{k_{1},k_{2}\}}}p_{\mathrm{BN}}(\mathbf{x}), (9) computed via np.einsum contractions following the
topological order of the network.
All 31 marginals normalise to 1 1 within machine precision
( < 10 − 14 <10^{-14} ).

The full constraint set consists of m 1 = 15 m_{1}{=}15 unary, m 2 = 13 m_{2}{=}13 binary, and m 3 = 3 m_{3}{=}3 ternary constraints,
expanded to m ≈ 280 m\approx 280 atomic indicator features.
For the held-out experiment ( section ˜ 5.2 ),
we use the 28 unary and binary constraints as training
and the 3 ternary constraints as held-out evaluation:
the ternary targets encode interactions strictly beyond the
solver’s training signal, providing a direct test of
generalisation to unseen higher-order interactions.

Code is available at https://github.com/mirko-degli-esposti/maxent-popsynth-pcd .
Full CPT specifications are reproduced in appendix ˜ A .

sections.heading: 5Experiments
sections.text:
All experiments use the GibbsPCDSolver implementation
described in section ˜ 3 , with Adam optimiser
( β 1 = 0.9 \beta_{1}{=}0.9 , β 2 = 0.999 \beta_{2}{=}0.999 , ε = 10 − 8 \varepsilon{=}10^{-8} )
and adaptive stopping according to eq. ˜ 8 with τ = 0.02 \tau{=}0.02 .
Unless otherwise noted, the persistent pool is initialised
uniformly and 𝝀 0 = 𝟎 \bm{\lambda}_{0}=\mathbf{0} .

As baseline, we use generalised raking (IPF) on an initial
synthetic sample of size N N drawn uniformly over the
attribute domains.
Its error is evaluated from the weighted frequency vector,
ensuring direct comparability with GibbsPCDSolver on the same
set of aggregate constraints.

sections.heading:
5.1Scaling Beyond Exact Enumeration
(K∈{12,20,30,40,50}K\in\{12,20,30,40,50\})
sections.text:
We begin by evaluating GibbsPCDSolver in the regime where
exact enumeration becomes infeasible.
This experiment directly tests the central claim of the paper:
that MaxEnt inference via PCD scales with N N and K K ,
rather than with | 𝒳 | |\mathcal{X}| .

More precisely, we evaluate GibbsPCDSolver across five WuGenerator instances
with K ∈ { 12 , 20 , 30 , 40 , 50 } K\in\{12,20,30,40,50\} , exclusively ternary
planted patterns, and | 𝒳 | |\mathcal{X}| ranging from 83 , 000 83{,}000 to 5.8 × 10 18 5.8{\times}10^{18} .
For K = 12 K{=}12 the exact L-BFGS solver is still available
and serves as reference; for K ≥ 20 K\geq 20 , | 𝒳 | |\mathcal{X}| is not
enumerable and GibbsPCDSolver is the only MaxEnt-based
method that can operate.
All runs use N = 100 , 000 N{=}100{,}000 , s = 5 s{=}5 , η = 0.01 \eta{=}0.01 ,
with Numba acceleration for K ≥ 20 K\geq 20 .
We compare against generalised raking at the same N N ,
using MRE, N eff N_{\mathrm{eff}} , and Shannon entropy H H as metrics. Table ˜ 4 reports the full results; fig. ˜ 4 summarises the key trends.

sections.heading: 5.2Held-Out Generalisation
sections.text:
We next evaluate whether the learned MaxEnt model captures
higher-order structure beyond the training constraints.
Both solvers are trained on unary and binary marginals,
and evaluated on held-out ternary interactions.

Both solvers are trained on the 28 unary and binary
constraints of Syn-ISTAT ( N = 100 , 000 N{=}100{,}000 );
the 3 ternary constraints are withheld and evaluated
after training.

sections.heading: 5.3Population Diversity
sections.text:
We now assess the diversity of the generated populations,
which is critical for downstream agent-based simulation.
Even when training accuracy is comparable, methods may differ
substantially in the variety of individuals they produce: a population
with correct marginals but low demographic diversity
produces unrealistic dynamics through excessive
cloning of a few archetypal profiles.
We evaluate diversity with three metrics:
effective sample size N eff N_{\mathrm{eff}} ( eq. ˜ 1 ),
empirical Shannon entropy H = − ∑ 𝐱 p ^ ​ ( 𝐱 ) ​ log ⁡ p ^ ​ ( 𝐱 ) H=-\sum_{\mathbf{x}}\hat{p}(\mathbf{x})\log\hat{p}(\mathbf{x}) ,
and number of distinct demographic profiles.

Table ˜ 6 reports results for both solvers
on the full Syn-ISTAT problem ( N = 100 , 000 N{=}100{,}000 , 31 constraints).

The two ratios in the bottom row of table ˜ 6 measure
different things and should not be confused.
Unique profiles counts the support of the pool before weighting:
raking retains 25 , 786 25{,}786 distinct tuples, but the vast majority
carry negligible weight and will almost never be drawn when
sampling a synthetic population. N eff N_{\mathrm{eff}} , by contrast, captures the effective concentration of
the weight distribution: the 22 × 22{\times} gap between the two
ratios ( 86.8 × 86.8{\times} vs. 3.8 × 3.8{\times} ) reflects the fact
that raking’s 25 , 786 25{,}786 unique profiles are “ghost” individuals
whose combined probability mass is negligible.
The relevant metric for agent-based simulation is N eff N_{\mathrm{eff}} :
it bounds the expected number of distinct archetypes in any
downstream sample, regardless of pool support.

The contrast is stark.
Raking converges to an effective population of 1 , 152 1{,}152 individuals: its weight distribution has Gini
coefficient 0.951 0.951 —exceeding the most unequal
empirical income distributions—and the Lorenz curve
(see fig. ˜ 5 ) departs dramatically from
equality.
GibbsPCDSolver maintains uniform pool weights by
construction, achieving N eff = N N_{\mathrm{eff}}{=}N with 97 , 025 97{,}025 distinct profiles ( 97 % 97\% of pool size) and an entropy 3.15 3.15 nats higher than raking.

The practical implication is direct.
For a synthetic municipality of n n inhabitants drawn
from the raking distribution, the expected number of
distinct profiles is ≈ N eff ≈ 1 , 200 \approx N_{\mathrm{eff}}\approx 1{,}200 regardless of n n : a city of 400 , 000 400{,}000 synthetic
Bolognesi would consist almost entirely of repetitions
of ∼ 1 , 200 \sim 1{,}200 archetypes.
GibbsPCDSolver provides the demographic variety that
agent-based urban models require for realistic
emergent behaviour.

This result provides the definitive answer to the
question of why MaxEnt inference via PCD is preferable
to raking even in settings where both achieve comparable
held-out accuracy: raking is algebraically exact on
training constraints but produces degenerate synthetic populations.

sections.heading: 5.4Hyperparameter Sensitivity: Wu Benchmark (K=8K{=}8)
sections.text:
We analyse the effect of key hyperparameters on controlled
problems where the exact MaxEnt solution is available.

More precisely, we characterise the effect of pool size N N and sweep count s s on two controlled problems where the exact solution is available:
one with binary constraints only and one with ternary constraints,
both on the same WuGenerator instance ( K = 8 K{=}8 , | 𝒳 | = 6 , 144 |\mathcal{X}|{=}6{,}144 ).

sections.heading: 5.5Hyperparameter Sensitivity: Syn-ISTAT (K=15K{=}15)
sections.text:
We finally examine hyperparameter sensitivity on the
Syn-ISTAT benchmark, where exact computation is not
available.
This experiment complements the Wu analysis by showing how
optimal settings depend on constraint structure in a more
realistic scenario: we repeat the sensitivity analysis on Syn-ISTAT,
where | 𝒳 | ≈ 1.7 × 10 8 |\mathcal{X}|\approx 1.7{\times}10^{8} and
the exact solver is unavailable.

sections.heading: 6.1Extending the Competence Map
sections.text:
Pachet and Zucker ( 2026 ) establish a competence map showing
that MaxEnt overtakes raking when K ≳ 28 K\gtrsim 28 and
ternary interactions are present.
Their map is computed under exact enumeration, which
limits its empirical coverage to K ≤ 40 K\leq 40 .
GibbsPCDSolver extends this boundary: since the cost
per gradient step is O ​ ( N ⋅ K ⋅ J ¯ ⋅ d max ) O(N\cdot K\cdot\bar{J}\cdot d_{\max}) and is independent of | 𝒳 | |\mathcal{X}| , the algorithm is in
principle applicable for any K K .

Our experiments ( section ˜ 5.1 ) provide data
points for K ∈ { 12 , 20 , 30 , 40 , 50 } K\in\{12,20,30,40,50\} , confirming
that GibbsPCDSolver converges reliably across five decades
of | 𝒳 | |\mathcal{X}| and that the accuracy–cost trade-off is
controlled by N N rather than K K .
At K = 40 K{=}40 , raking fails with MRE = 0.129 \mathrm{MRE}{=}0.129 , consistent
with the Pachet–Zucker threshold; at K = 50 K{=}50 , | 𝒳 | ≈ 6 × 10 18 |\mathcal{X}|\approx 6{\times}10^{18} and GibbsPCDSolver
remains the only MaxEnt-based method that can operate,
achieving MRE = 0.013 \mathrm{MRE}{=}0.013 .

Our experiments ( section ˜ 5.1 ) provide data
points for K ∈ { 12 , 20 , 30 , 40 , 50 } K\in\{12,20,30,40,50\} , confirming
that GibbsPCDSolver converges reliably across five decades
of | 𝒳 | |\mathcal{X}| and that the accuracy–cost trade-off is
controlled by N N rather than K K .
At K = 40 K{=}40 , raking fails with MRE = 0.129 \mathrm{MRE}{=}0.129 , consistent
with the Pachet–Zucker threshold; at K = 50 K{=}50 , | 𝒳 | ≈ 6 × 10 18 |\mathcal{X}|\approx 6{\times}10^{18} and GibbsPCDSolver
remains the only MaxEnt-based method that can operate,
achieving MRE = 0.013 \mathrm{MRE}{=}0.013 .
This statement is scoped to the aggregate-only regime:
deep generative models can in principle operate at
large K K , but require individual-level microdata for
training ( section ˜ 2.3 ), which are often unavailable in many national census.

sections.heading: 6.2When to Use Which Method
sections.text:
The experimental results, combined with the diversity
analysis, suggest the following practical decision rule.
For the primary use case of this paper—synthetic
population generation for agent-based urban
simulation—the recommendation is unambiguous and
holds at any scale:

• Any K K , downstream agent-based simulation :
prefer GibbsPCDSolver over raking regardless of K K .
Raking’s N eff N_{\mathrm{eff}} degradation is structural and
grows with K K : from N eff / N = 7.1 % N_{\mathrm{eff}}/N{=}7.1\% at K = 12 K{=}12 to 0.12 % 0.12\% at K ≥ 40 K\geq 40 , making the synthesised
population unsuitable for behavioural simulation
regardless of training MRE.
GibbsPCDSolver maintains N eff = N N_{\mathrm{eff}}{=}N by construction
at all scales. • K ≤ 12 K\leq 12 , | 𝒳 | ≲ 10 7 |\mathcal{X}|\lesssim 10^{7} :
use exact L-BFGS (Pachet and Zucker, 2026 ) .
Enumeration is feasible, the solution is exact,
and runtime is under 3 3 s. • K ∈ [ 12 , 30 ] K\in[12,30] , mixed or ternary arities :
use GibbsPCDSolver with N = 50 , 000 N{=}50{,}000 – 100 , 000 100{,}000 , s = 3 s{=}3 – 5 5 , η = 0.01 \eta{=}0.01 .
Exact enumeration is borderline or impossible;
GibbsPCDSolver achieves MRE ≈ 0.010 \mathrm{MRE}\approx 0.010 – 0.019 0.019 with runtime of 7 7 – 16 16 minutes at N = 100 , 000 N{=}100{,}000 . • K ≥ 40 K\geq 40 :
use GibbsPCDSolver with N ≥ 100 , 000 N\geq 100{,}000 , s = 5 s{=}5 , and Numba acceleration.
Raking fails to satisfy training constraints
in this regime ( MRE ≥ 0.065 \mathrm{MRE}\geq 0.065 in our experiments)
and simultaneously degenerates to N eff ≈ 120 N_{\mathrm{eff}}\approx 120 effective individuals.

sections.heading: 6.3Hyperparameter Sensitivity and Calibration
sections.text:
The main practical finding is that the optimal
learning rate η \eta and sweep count s s depend on
the complexity of the constraint graph, not on K K alone.
The key indicator is the presence of high-arity
constraints connecting high-degree nodes: when such
constraints are present, the stochastic gradient has
higher variance and slower mixing, requiring smaller η \eta and more sweeps per step.
A principled diagnostic is to monitor the trajectory
of 𝝀 t \bm{\lambda}_{t} across iterations: sustained oscillation
of the sequence ( 𝝀 t ) (\bm{\lambda}_{t}) signals that η \eta is
too large, while monotone but slow descent of MRE
signals that s s is too small.
Note that this is a diagnostic on the dynamics of 𝝀 t \bm{\lambda}_{t} , not on its distance from 𝝀 ∗ \bm{\lambda}^{*} :
as discussed in section ˜ 3.4 , the latter
is not a meaningful convergence metric in the presence
of unary constraints.
In practice, monitoring MRE directly is more reliable
than monitoring ‖ Δ ​ 𝝀 t ‖ \|\Delta\bm{\lambda}_{t}\| for both
diagnostics.

sections.heading: 7Conclusion
sections.text:
We have presented two contributions to scalable maximum
entropy population synthesis from aggregate census data.

GibbsPCDSolver replaces the intractable exact
expectation step of Pachet and Zucker ( 2026 ) with a Persistent
Contrastive Divergence estimate from a persistent Gibbs
pool—a minimal modification (one line of Algorithm 1)
that removes the | 𝒳 | |\mathcal{X}| barrier limiting exact MaxEnt
to K ≲ 20 K\lesssim 20 attributes.
Scaling experiments across K ∈ { 12 , 20 , 30 , 40 , 50 } K\in\{12,20,30,40,50\} confirm that GibbsPCDSolver maintains MRE ∈ [ 0.010 , 0.018 ] \mathrm{MRE}\in[0.010,0.018] while | 𝒳 | |\mathcal{X}| grows from 83 , 000 83{,}000 to 5.8 × 10 18 5.8\times 10^{18} —eighteen orders
of magnitude—with runtime scaling as O ​ ( K ) O(K) rather
than O ​ ( | 𝒳 | ) O(|\mathcal{X}|) .
At K = 40 K{=}40 , raking fails to satisfy training constraints
( MRE = 0.129 \mathrm{MRE}{=}0.129 ) and simultaneously degenerates to N eff ≈ 120 N_{\mathrm{eff}}\approx 120 effective individuals;
GibbsPCDSolver remains accurate and maintains full
pool diversity at both K = 40 K{=}40 and K = 50 K{=}50 .

Syn-ISTAT is a K = 15 K{=}15 Italian demographic
benchmark with analytically exact marginal targets
derived from ISTAT-inspired conditional probability
tables, a training/held-out split that tests
generalisation to third-order interactions, and | 𝒳 | ≈ 1.7 × 10 8 |\mathcal{X}|\approx 1.7\times 10^{8} — placing it
firmly in the non-enumerable regime.
On this benchmark, GibbsPCDSolver converges to MRE = 0.030 \mathrm{MRE}{=}0.030 on training constraints and outperforms
raking on the held-out ternary constraint involving
the highest-degree node in the constraint graph,
where the coherent exponential family parametrisation
exploits multi-hop structure that sequential IPF
rescaling cannot capture.

The most consequential finding, however, is not
about accuracy: GibbsPCDSolver produces populations
with effective sample size 86.8 × 86.8{\times} larger and
entropy 3.15 3.15 nats higher than generalised raking.
Raking’s weight collapse is structural and grows
monotonically with K K —from N eff / N = 7.1 % N_{\mathrm{eff}}/N{=}7.1\% at K = 12 K{=}12 to 0.12 % 0.12\% at K ≥ 40 K\geq 40 —rendering the
synthesised population unsuitable for agent-based
simulation regardless of training MRE.
For synthetic population generation intended for
urban digital twins and agent-based models,
GibbsPCDSolver is the method of choice at any
scale: not because it achieves lower training error,
but because it produces the demographic diversity
that realistic emergent dynamics require.

sections.heading: Generative AI Disclosure
sections.text:
The author used Claude (Anthropic) as an AI assistant during
the preparation of this manuscript, primarily for LaTeX editing,
consistency checking across sections, and refinement of
mathematical notation.
All scientific content, experimental results, and conclusions
are the sole responsibility of the author.

sections.heading: Acknowledgements
sections.text:
The author thanks François Pachet (ImagineAllThePeople)
for stimulating discussions on maximum entropy population
synthesis and for making available a preprint of Pachet and Zucker ( 2026 ) prior to publication.

sections.heading: A.2Unary Marginals
sections.text:
Anchor variables (italicised) are drawn from fixed marginals;
non-anchor marginals are derived as implied marginals from the
binary/ternary tables by marginalisation over the conditioning
variable (see section ˜ 4 ).
When a variable appears in more than one table, implied marginals
are averaged and renormalised; the maximum pairwise discrepancy
across sources is 0.025 0.025 (employment), which is within acceptable
tolerance for the MaxEnt solver.

sections.heading: A.3Binary Contingency Tables
sections.text:
All 13 binary tables specify P ​ ( B ∣ A ) P(B\mid A) ; each row sums to 1.
The conditioning variable A A is always an anchor or a variable
with a well-established prior; B B is the conditioned attribute.

sections.heading: A.4Ternary Contingency Tables
sections.text:
Three ternary constraints capture higher-order interactions that
cannot be factored into pairwise terms without significant
distortion.
Each ternary table P ​ ( C ∣ A , B ) P(C\mid A,B) nominally supersedes the
corresponding binary tables P ​ ( C ∣ A ) P(C\mid A) and P ​ ( C ∣ B ) P(C\mid B) ,
but both are retained in the constraint set to maintain a richer
constraint graph topology.

sections.heading: A.5Constraint Graph
sections.text:
With the 13 binary and 3 ternary constraints, all 15 variables
are connected; no variable is isolated from the graph.
High-degree nodes are age (degree 7: marital, employment,
diet, alcohol, activity, plus ternaries T1 and T3) and employment (degree 5: age, education, income, commute,
sex, plus T1 and T3).
Degree-1 nodes (connected to exactly one other variable) are:
marital (age only), has children (household size only),
commute time (employment only), diet type (age only),
physical activity (age only), and alcohol use (age only).
This topology motivates the held-out experiment in section ˜ 5.2 : employment’s high degree makes T3
( sex × age → employment \text{sex}\times\text{age}\to\text{employment} )
the most demanding held-out constraint, and the one on which
GibbsPCDSolver outperforms raking.

figures.caption:
Figure 1: Experiment A0 ( K = 6 K{=}6 , | 𝒳 | = 216 |\mathcal{X}|{=}216 ). Left : MRE convergence curves for four pool sizes
(log scale); dashed line is exact L-BFGS reference. Centre : scatter of 𝝀 ^ \hat{\bm{\lambda}} vs. 𝝀 ∗ \bm{\lambda}^{*} at N = 10 , 000 N{=}10{,}000 ; the systematic offset from the diagonal
reflects a gauge degree of freedom in 𝝀 \bm{\lambda} (see footnote),
not solver error. Right : estimated frequencies α ^ j \hat{\alpha}_{j} vs. targets α j \alpha_{j} at N = 10 , 000 N{=}10{,}000 ;
points lie close to the diagonal across the full
range [ 0.004 , 0.906 ] [0.004,\,0.906] .

Figure 2: Experiment A1a ( K = 8 K{=}8 , | 𝒳 | = 6 , 144 |\mathcal{X}|{=}6{,}144 , m = 61 m{=}61 , s = 5 s{=}5 ). Left : MRE convergence curves for four pool
sizes (log scale); dashed line is the exact
L-BFGS reference ( MRE = 4 × 10 − 5 \mathrm{MRE}{=}4{\times}10^{-5} ). Centre : scatter of 𝝀 MCMC \bm{\lambda}_{\mathrm{MCMC}} vs. 𝝀 ∗ \bm{\lambda}^{*} at N = 50 , 000 N{=}50{,}000 ; the constant
offset from the diagonal is the gauge degree of
freedom induced by unary constraints and does not
affect the distribution p 𝝀 p_{\bm{\lambda}} . Right : final MRE vs. N N (log–log);
the dashed reference line follows 1 / N 1/\sqrt{N} ,
confirming the theoretical variance floor.

Figure 3: Experiment A1b ( K = 10 K{=}10 , | 𝒳 | = 59 , 049 |\mathcal{X}|{=}59{,}049 , m = 30 m{=}30 binary constraints, s = 5 s{=}5 ). Left : KL divergence KL ​ ( p 𝝀 ∗ ∥ p 𝝀 MCMC ) \mathrm{KL}(p_{\bm{\lambda}^{*}}\|p_{\bm{\lambda}_{\mathrm{MCMC}}}) (red, left axis) and relative parameter distance ‖ Δ ​ 𝝀 ‖ / ‖ 𝝀 ∗ ‖ \|\Delta\bm{\lambda}\|/\|\bm{\lambda}^{*}\| (blue, right axis)
vs. pool size N N (log–log); the dotted line
shows the O ​ ( 1 / N ) O(1/N) reference. Centre : scatter of 𝝀 MCMC \bm{\lambda}_{\mathrm{MCMC}} vs. 𝝀 ∗ \bm{\lambda}^{*} at N = 50 , 000 N{=}50{,}000 ; unlike A0–A1a,
no gauge offset is present and points cluster
tightly around the diagonal
( ‖ Δ ​ 𝝀 ‖ / ‖ 𝝀 ∗ ‖ = 0.016 \|\Delta\bm{\lambda}\|/\|\bm{\lambda}^{*}\|{=}0.016 ). Right : estimated constraint frequencies α ^ j \hat{\alpha}_{j} vs. exact targets α j ∗ \alpha^{*}_{j} at N = 50 , 000 N{=}50{,}000 , for binary constraints (blue).

Figure 4: A2 scaling experiments ( N = 100 , 000 N{=}100{,}000 , arity=3). Left : MRE of GibbsPCDSolver (solid blue) vs. exact MaxEnt of Pachet and Zucker ( 2026 ) (dashed blue,
their Table 2).
The two curves use different problem instances
(WuGenerator vs. NPORS); the comparison is
qualitative—both achieve MRE in the 1 1 – 2 % 2\% range—and is not a claim that GibbsPCDSolver
outperforms exact enumeration.
At K = 30 K{=}30 only GibbsPCDSolver is available
( | 𝒳 | ≈ 5 × 10 11 |\mathcal{X}|\approx 5{\times}10^{11} ). Centre : raking N eff / N N_{\mathrm{eff}}/N (red diamonds,
log scale, left axis) and Shannon entropy H H (dotted lines, right axis) for both methods. Right : fit time vs. K K (log scale).

Figure 5: Population diversity: GibbsPCDSolver vs. generalised
raking ( N = 100 , 000 N{=}100{,}000 , Syn-ISTAT, K = 15 K{=}15 ,
full training on 31 constraints). Left : raking weight distribution (log scale);
the dashed line marks the uniform weight 1 / N 1/N of
GibbsPCDSolver.
After convergence, raking concentrates almost all mass
on N eff = 1 , 152 N_{\rm eff}{=}1{,}152 effective individuals
( 1.2 % 1.2\% of N N ). Centre : Lorenz curve of raking weights
(Gini = 0.951 {=}0.951 ); the diagonal is perfect equality.
The shaded area between the two curves quantifies
the weight concentration. Right : normalised diversity metrics for both
solvers; ratios above each pair indicate the
GibbsPCD advantage.
GibbsPCDSolver maintains uniform pool weights by
construction ( N eff = N N_{\rm eff}{=}N ), achieving an 86.8 × 86.8{\times} diversity advantage in effective
sample size and 3.15 3.15 nats higher Shannon entropy
than raking—essential for realistic agent-based
dynamics.

Figure 6: A-ISTAT-3 — pool-size sensitivity on Syn-ISTAT
( K = 15 K{=}15 , | 𝒳 | ≈ 1.7 × 10 8 |\mathcal{X}|{\approx}1.7{\times}10^{8} ,
31 constraints, s = 5 s{=}5 , η = 0.01 \eta{=}0.01 ). Left : training MRE vs. pool size N N (log–log);
the dotted line is the theoretical variance floor 1 / ( 2 ​ N ) 1/(2\sqrt{N}) ; observed MRE lies above the floor
at all N N , confirming that mixing bias dominates
over gradient variance in this regime. Right : Pareto frontier of MRE vs. fit time;
colour encodes log 10 ⁡ ( N ) \log_{10}(N) .
The dashed vertical line marks the fit time of
generalised raking ( 29 29 s); raking achieves MRE train = 0 \mathrm{MRE}_{\text{train}}{=}0 by algebraic construction
( † \dagger ) and is not directly comparable to the

constraints.
